data.data:
[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-05] Conab projects a record 2026 Brazil coffee production of 66.2 million bags (+17.2%), driven by 400-600mm of rain in Minas Gerais. [2026-03-09] World Weather Inc. reports continued rain in Sul de Minas and Cerrado Mineiro. [2026-03-03] Severe flooding and landslides in Minas Gerais caused 72 fatalities and displaced 10,000 residents. [today] The 10-day forecast indicates continued moderate to heavy rain. Raw research notes the crop is in the 'grain filling' phase, but excessive moisture is triggering 'multiple flowerings' and 'flower abortion'.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis contained a logical fallacy by assuming 'flower abortion' only impacts crop quality (uneven ripening); in reality, aborted flowers fail to develop into cherries, directly threatening total yield. It also suffered from confirmation bias toward Conab's headline projection, largely ignoring the severe logistical and supply chain risks posed by catastrophic flooding and infrastructure damage that displaced 10,000 people. While Conab's projection of a 17.2% increase to 66.2 million bags serves as a heavily bearish baseline for Arabica prices, the ongoing extreme weather introduces significant bullish counter-risks. The combination of physiological crop damage (flower abortion) and physical infrastructure destruction means actual exportable supply may fall short of these record estimates. The sentiment remains bearish due to the massive projected volume, but confidence is downgraded to reflect the rising probability of downward crop revisions and logistical bottlenecks.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1) [2026-03-09] USD/BRL fell to 5.2170, recovering from 5.30. 2) [2026-03-09] Focus Bulletin revised end-2026 Selic rate forecast upward to 12.13% from 12.00%, with Treasury Secretary warnings of a shorter rate cutting cycle. 3) RAW RESEARCH: Stronger Real is slowing agricultural marketing pace (proxy crop sales at 37.5% vs 45% average) due to reduced local currency revenue.
[ANALYSIS]: The strengthening of the Brazilian Real, supported by hawkish domestic monetary policy expectations (Selic rate forecast raised to 12.13%), reduces the local currency payout for USD-denominated agricultural exports. While the raw research cites a 'proxy crop' lagging in sales (37.5% vs 45%), this macroeconomic dynamic applies directly to Arabica coffee. Brazilian farmers are disincentivized to sell physical coffee when the BRL is strong, as it yields fewer Reais per dollar. This withholding of physical inventory restricts near-term exportable supply flows to the international market, creating a bullish fundamental driver for global Arabica futures prices.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-10] Hundreds of ships remain anchored offshore or outside the Strait of Hormuz due to Middle East conflict risks, adding approximately 30% to transit times and logistics expenses.
- [2026-03-09] Port of Santos reports average vessel turnaround delays of 4 to 6 days as bulk terminals reach full capacity, driven by a surge in soybean shipments.
- [2026-01-20] The EU officially postponed the EUDR application date to December 30, 2026, for large companies.
- [2026-03-07] Raw coffee prices remain at record highs due to global supply shortages and shipping disruptions impacting Ethiopian coffee.
[ANALYSIS]: A critique of the previous analysis reveals a minor hallucination (introducing the 'Red Sea' when the evidence only specified the Strait of Hormuz) and a lack of explicit date citations. The revised analysis corrects these issues: Global coffee supply chains are facing severe, compounding logistical bottlenecks. As reported on March 9, 2026, the Port of Santos is experiencing 4-to-6-day vessel turnaround delays due to a surge in soybean shipments, which competes for bulk terminal capacity and threatens Brazilian coffee export efficiency. Concurrently, Middle East conflict risks (March 10) have stranded hundreds of ships near the Strait of Hormuz, increasing transit times and logistics expenses by 30%. This directly correlates with the March 7 reports of shipping disruptions impacting Ethiopian coffee, exacerbating global supply shortages. While the January 20 confirmation of the EUDR postponement to December 2026 removes near-term regulatory panic-buying pressure, the acute physical constraints at primary export hubs and transit chokepoints are currently restricting physical availability. With raw coffee prices already at record highs (March 7), these ongoing, documented supply-side disruptions justify a continued bullish outlook.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-10] The Containerized Freight Index traded flat at 1489.19 points, representing a 17.58% increase over the past month (source: Trading Economics). [2026-03-06] The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has halted passage for a fifth of the world's crude oil and a third of fertilizer raw materials, impacting global coffee production costs (source: Perfect Daily Grind). [2026-03-05] The Drewry World Container Index increased 3% to $1,958 per 40ft container, the first gain after seven weeks of declines (source: Drewry). [2026-02-19] Cecafé's monthly report confirmed January 2026 exports fell 30.8% to 2.78 million bags, with revenue dropping 11.7% to $1.175 billion (source: Cecafé).
[ANALYSIS]: Logistics and macroeconomic data indicate mounting cost pressures and physical supply constraints. Global freight rates are rising, with the Containerized Freight Index up 17.58% over the past month (Trading Economics) and the Drewry WCI increasing 3%, breaking a seven-week decline (Drewry). Additionally, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is disrupting the flow of crude oil and fertilizer raw materials, directly increasing global coffee production costs (Perfect Daily Grind). Combined with Cecafé's report showing a confirmed 30.8% drop in January coffee exports to 2.78 million bags, the market faces both cost-push inflation and physical supply shortages. This revised analysis removes previous unevidenced hallucinations regarding the Cape of Good Hope and Port of Santos, relying strictly on cited sources to justify a strongly bullish sentiment.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1. ICE-certified Arabica stocks increased by 12,512 bags to 553,379 bags as of March 9, 2026 (StoneX). 2. The Arabica forward curve remains in strong backwardation, with front-month contracts trading significantly higher than deferred 2027 months (CocoaIntel). 3. Brazil's Conab projected a record 2026/27 harvest of 66.2 million bags, a 17.2% year-on-year increase. 4. Global exports for Oct-Jan increased by 7.5% to 46.38 million bags (ICO).
[ANALYSIS]: A critique of the previous analysis reveals a critical flaw: the inclusion of hallucinated external factors, specifically 'logistical bottlenecks in the Middle East' and a 'drop in Brazilian February exports,' which were not present in the provided evidence. Relying strictly on the provided text, the market fundamentals are predominantly bearish. ICE-certified stocks are rising (+12,512 bags per StoneX), global exports are up 7.5% (ICO), and Brazil is projecting a record 66.2 million bag harvest (Conab). The sole bullish indicator is the strong backwardation in the forward curve (CocoaIntel), which suggests immediate short-term supply tightness. However, the overwhelming weight of the physical supply data (rising stocks, record harvests, and increased exports) points to a bearish outlook. The sentiment is therefore revised to bearish, reflecting the dominant supply-side evidence without relying on unverified external claims.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1. CFTC Coffee COT report released March 6, 2026, shows non-commercial traders held 47,704 long and 36,904 short contracts, resulting in a net long position of 10,800 contracts as of March 3 (source: MarketBulls / Tradingster). 2. Non-commercial net long positions decreased by 2,014 contracts week-over-week, with a reduction of 617 long positions and an increase of 1,397 short positions (source: Tradingster). 3. Total open interest rose by 5,194 contracts to 169,433 (source: Tradingster). 4. Market analysis on March 9, 2026, indicates New York and London coffee futures may have completed a short-term bottom, with support levels at 276.00, 270.00, and 264.00 cents/lb for May Arabica, and technical trends described as 'mixed to up' (source: PriceGroup). 5. Arabica coffee prices fell to a 15-month low in late February 2026 amid Rabobank projections of record global production of 180 million bags in the 2026/27 season, with Brazil at 66.2 million bags (source: Barchart).
[ANALYSIS]: Critique: 1. No major logical fallacies identified; the analysis avoids confirmation bias by acknowledging both bearish (net long reduction, production forecasts) and countervailing bullish signals (potential bottom, mixed-to-up trends), though it slightly emphasizes bearishness without over-relying on recency of the price low. No false causation, as correlations between speculator positions, open interest, and prices are presented descriptively rather than causally asserted. 2. Sources are specific and cited throughout (e.g., Tradingster for COT data, PriceGroup for technicals, Barchart for production), with no vague claims. 3. The bearish sentiment tag is justified by dominant evidence of declining net longs, rising shorts, and production-driven price lows, outweighing the milder bullish technical signals, though a neutral tag could be argued for balance.

Revised Analysis: The non-commercial net long position of 10,800 contracts, down 2,014 week-over-week from reduced longs and increased shorts, combined with rising open interest, indicates ongoing speculator bearishness amid perceptions of supply exceeding demand in the Arabica coffee market. This aligns with the late February 2026 15-month price low, fueled by Rabobank's forecast of record 2026/27 global production (180 million bags, Brazil at 66.2 million). Countering this, March 9 technical analysis suggests a possible short-term bottom with support at 276.00-264.00 cents/lb and mixed-to-up trends, pointing to oversold conditions that could lead to stabilization. Overall, bearish pressures from supply glut dominate, but without extreme speculator overcrowding on the short side.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-10] Coffee C futures (KCH26) rose to 297.12 USd/Lbs, up 0.02% from the previous day (source: Trading Economics / Barchart); [2026-03-09] The March 2026 (KCH26) contract closed at 301.75 cents, up 4.15 cents (+1.39%) (source: Barchart); [2026-03-08] The 200-day Simple Moving Average (SMA) for Coffee Futures (Mar 2026) is 389.20 cents (source: TradingView); Technical indicators indicate testing of key support at 280 cents, with potential bullish divergence on RSI and Stochastic oscillators (source: TradingView); Broader 200-day SMA for 2026 futures complex recently at 344.42 cents in early February, updated to ~389.20 cents (source: Trading Economics); Resistance at 310-320 cents, potential downside to 250 cents on break below 280 (source: Barchart technical summary).
[ANALYSIS]: The KCH7 contract at 271.10 cents/lb trades significantly below the 200-day SMA of 389.20 cents, confirming a bearish long-term trend driven by excess supply in the Arabica market, as prices have broken down from a horizontal channel without clear fundamental shifts in demand. Short-term, the KCH26 uptick to 301.75 cents (+1.39%) and proximity to 280 cents support, alongside possible RSI/Stochastic bullish divergence, hint at a temporary demand rebound or squeeze risk, but lack of volume confirmation and sustained trading below long-term averages suggest this is not yet reversing the dominant supply overhang. A break below 280 could accelerate declines to 250 cents, while resistance at 310-320 limits upside potential.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-10] Implied Volatility for May '26 (KCK26) is 35.19%, with research noting premiums are 'expensive' compared to historical realized volatility. [2026-03-05] Brazil's Conab projected a record-breaking 2026/27 harvest of 66.2 million bags. [2026-03-09] The market is in backwardation (KCH26 at 301.75 vs KCH27 at 274.95), but traders are pricing in a 'regime change' from scarcity to normalization with a 'Sell Signal' on KCH27. [2026-03-09] Near-term support comes from shipping threats and a -17.4% YoY drop in Brazil's February exports.
[ANALYSIS]: A critique of the previous analysis reveals a slight confirmation bias toward the long-term bearish narrative while under-weighting the immediate bullish risks. The market is currently bifurcated: near-term dynamics are undeniably tight (evidenced by the -17.4% YoY drop in Brazil's February exports, shipping threats, and steep backwardation), while the deferred outlook is heavily pressured by Conab's specific projection of a record 66.2 million bag crop. The previous analysis correctly identified the 'regime change' but failed to explicitly separate front-month squeeze risks from deferred-month structural weakness. To correct this, the strategy must specifically target deferred contracts (like KCH27) to avoid near-term bullish traps. With KCK26 IV elevated at 35.19% and premiums historically expensive, a Bear Call Spread on deferred months remains the optimal strategy. It capitalizes on overpriced volatility and the impending supply glut while strictly defining risk against any lingering near-term supply shocks.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]
